data.title: 'Transformative' HIV PrEP shot slowly overcomes cost hurdles
data.description:
HIV/AIDS activists heralded the approval of a new twice-yearly pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) medication last year, but these celebrations were tempered by insurers’ reluctance to cover its exorbitant cost. 

In the months since, payers are coming around, and more patients have access to this landmark advancement. 

In June, the Food and Drug Administration approved Yeztugo, an injection of the drug lenacapavir, as a form of HIV PrEP. The drug is manufactured by Gilead Sciences. PrEP medication comprises preventative medications for HIV-negative people at high risk of exposure.

Carlos del Rio, co-director of the Emory Center for AIDS Research, called the approval “transformative” at the time for its potential to boost PrEP uptake. 

But with a list price of nearly $30,000 annually, insurers did not immediately add the drug to their formularies. By comparison, the other long-acting HIV PrEP injectable medication, Apretude, has an annual cost of roughly $22,000. Oral PrEP options are significantly cheaper, though adherence may be less strict as they are meant to be taken daily. 

CVS Health became the face of this hesitancy when it said in August it would not be covering Yeztugo, citing financial and regulatory factors. Reporting at the time, however, suggested negotiations were still ongoing between CVS and Gilead.

Talks between the two companies appear to have been productive, with CVS walking back its decision earlier this month. 

“The speed of payer uptake is very encouraging, and the latest news we can share is that CVS has confirmed they now cover Yeztugo,” a Gilead spokesperson told The Hill in a statement. 

But the company still registered discontent with the cost.

“We were able to negotiate price concessions for our clients from Gilead, and Yeztugo is now covered on CVS Caremark’s standard commercial formularies. Yeztugo’s net price, however, remains far too high given the presence of several highly effective pills and injections for PrEP already on the market, at far lower prices,” a CVS spokesperson said in a statement.

According to HIV activist groups, CVS Caremark, the pharmacy benefit manager (PBM) for CVS Health, is trailing behind other major payers. CVS Caremark is one of the three largest PBMs in the U.S. 

The HIV+Hepatitis Policy Institute stated in a letter in September that the other two largest PBMs, Express Scripts and Optum Rx, had already added Yeztugo to their formularies by that point. When The Hill reached out to Optum Rx in September, a company spokesperson said at the time that “a final recommendation on formulary placement has not yet been made.”

On Tuesday, Optum indicated Yeztugo’s cost is keeping it off the formulary.

“We offer an oral generic and a long-acting injectable therapy option for the indication of HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis on both our premium and select formularies. Yeztugo’s net cost was higher, so currently it is excluded on our premium formulary and on tier 3 of our select formulary,” a spokesperson for Optum said in a statement to The Hill.

These PBMs service many of the top health insurers, including Cigna, UnitedHealthcare and some Blue Cross Blue Shield plans. CVS Caremark acts as the PBM for Aetna, which it owns. Together, these three PBMs control about 80 percent of the U.S. market.

Carl Schmid, executive director of the HIV+Hepatitis Policy Institute, said he isn’t surprised CVS eventually came around, noting CVS Caremark was listed as an authorized distributor of Yeztugo even before it agreed to cover it. Schmid’s group had argued it was a legal imperative to cover the drug.

Under the Affordable Care Act (ACA), health insurers must cover preventive care services that receive a grade A or B from the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force, something the Supreme Court upheld last year around the same time that Yeztugo was approved. 

HIV PrEP services have a grade A from the task force. But the most recent update to the recommendation was made in 2023, predating Yeztugo’s approval and leaving ambiguity over whether the new drug was retroactively included in the decision.

“I’m glad they’re following the law and they’re doing what they’re supposed to be doing,” said Shmid. “Now we have to make sure that people know about it.” 

According to Gilead, the majority of U.S. insurers have now agreed to cover Yeztugo. 

“The Yeztugo launch is off to a strong start, and we’re very pleased with the broad insurance coverage – now more than 85%. This is extraordinary and it enables the rapid access and uptake that will eliminate HIV. CVS communicated their normal review process of 6+ months. They have now reviewed Yeztugo and we are pleased to share that they have added it to their formulary” as of Jan. 1, a Gilead spokesperson said. 

While private insurers took their time in considering whether to cover this latest HIV preventive drug, there was one cohort in the U.S. who assuredly received coverage: those on Medicaid and ACA marketplace plans.

Now that major private insurers are agreeing to cover Yeztugo, Shmid said the next challenge is to ensure patient accessibility.

He noted that unlike oral PrEP pills, which can be picked up from a pharmacy and taken at home, injectable PrEP must be administered by a clinician, which presents a barrier of access for many patients whose plans may not cover office visits. 

“Just because you’re covering it doesn’t equate to access. We still have people being charged cost-sharing. People are still being subject to prior authorization,” Schmid said. “We don’t want any prior authorizations. And then also, people are charged for their medical visits in the labs as well, and they’re supposed to be free.”

data.title: The Challenger disaster, 40 years on, and the speech that consoled the nation
data.description:
On this day 40 years ago, a generation of schoolchildren learned that the future can break your heart.

That morning, television carts had been rolled into classrooms and assemblies across the country. We fidgeted and waited to watch Christa McAuliffe, a social studies teacher from Concord, N.H., board Space Shuttle Challenger and become the first teacher in space. Then, at 11:40 a.m., just 73 seconds after liftoff, the shuttle broke apart into a plume of white smoke against the blue Cape Canaveral sky.

Minutes later, Pat Buchanan, then the White House communications director, walked into the Oval Office to tell President Ronald Reagan what had happened. Reagan was scheduled to deliver his State of the Union that night in primetime. It was to be a triumphant moment. But no longer.

I was a kindergartener, but I can still recall the shock and confusion of that day. What I did not know then is that I was about to see the speech that would one day inspire me to become a speechwriter.

Tributes to Reagan’s gifts as the Great Communicator often take us to the windswept cliffs above Normandy Beach. There, in 1984, he marked the 40th anniversary of D-Day with the aging “Boys of Pointe du Hoc” seated before him. “But they’ll be there, they’ll be there,” an advance man had told speechwriter Peggy Noonan. It was the ultimate fusion of rhetoric and stagecraft.

But for me, the Challenger speech will always be the one that sets the standard. Yes, it is the quintessential “consoler-in-chief” moment. But crafted in a race against the clock, it is a pure distillation of the things we hold dear — a tribute to struggle, discovery, and resolve. 

At 5 p.m., Reagan spoke from the Oval Office to a single camera in a solemn space, instead of on the grand stage he had expected to take later that evening. Another change: He refrained from the grand salutation to “fellow Americans,” beginning with “ladies and gentlemen,” in the style of an emcee announcing a change in the program. He spoke of being “pained to the core” — words someone at the National Security Council had tried to cut, as if to edit out the anguish — and invoked our “national loss.”

Then — and this is the thing I always come back to — he addressed the mourners in turn. He began with the families, extending condolences and immortalizing their loved ones not as victims, but as pioneers.

Then he spoke to us directly, the “schoolchildren of America,” offering reassurance in plain language. “I know it’s hard to understand,” he said, “but sometimes painful things like this happen.” He gave us permission to be sad without being afraid.

To the men and women of NASA, he spoke of first principles: “We don’t hide our space program. We don’t keep secrets and cover things up. We do it all up front and in public. That’s the way freedom is.”

And to the world, he made clear that America would endure and continue on in space.

Having traced that arc of shared grief, Reagan ended with verses that have echoed across four decades. Noonan put it in the president’s wheelhouse with the words of John Gillespie Magee, Jr., a pilot killed during World War II, to say the astronauts had “slipped the surly bonds of earth” to “touch the face of God.”

The Challenger speech spoke to us because it spoke for us. Reagan addressed distinct pain with distinct care, and specificity became unity. It was segmentation without fragmentation.

We have, by and large, forgotten how to do this. Today, we slice audiences into bases and micro-targets, leveraging data to tell each tribe what it wants to hear. The result is rhetoric that flatters the faction but starves the whole. So it is good we have this artifact to remind us of a time when the president’s primary job was to bind a nation.

There is a postscript worth noting. The Great Communicator wasn’t sure he had met the moment. He had been vulnerable; he had the performer’s doubt. It wasn’t until a call from Frank Sinatra later that night that Reagan finally felt at ease.

Forty years on, we remember the seven who were lost. We should also remember the spirit that carried us through that day. We settle for the comfort of the silo, but we do not have to hide from the hard things, and the call of Americans is not to look away, but to look up.

Michael Ricci, a partner in the communications firm Seven Letter, served in senior communications roles on Capitol Hill and as director of communications for Maryland Governor Larry Hogan (R).

data.title: Gerber issues nationwide recall: Things to know
data.description:
NEW YORK (WPIX) – Major baby foods manufacturer Gerber has announced a nationwide recall over the possibility of two unintended ingredients — soft plastic and paper pieces.

According to the voluntary recall, limited batches of Gerber Arrowroot Biscuits could contain either the soft plastic or both foreign materials.

Contamination of the batches, which were produced between July 2025 and September 2025, was apparently traced back to an arrowroot flour supplier that initiated its own recall.

The following batch codes and best-before dates of affected products are:

Credit: Gerber Products Company

Products should be returned to the place of purchase for a refund.

The company states that no illnesses or injuries have been reported.

“The quality, safety and integrity of our products remain our highest priority, and we take this responsibility seriously,” Gerber said in a news release. “We sincerely apologize for any concerns or inconvenience this action causes for parents, caregivers and retail customers.” 

For customer support and questions, contact Gerber at 1-800-443-7237.

data.title: 5 questions about Trump's ban on investors buying homes
data.description:
President Trump’s executive order to prevent Wall Street investors from purchasing single-family homes is the administration’s most ambitious attempt to address the state of the housing market. 

The president argued that Wall Street investors have bought a “growing share” of single-family homes and directed his administration to take steps to crack down on the practice. 

“Hardworking young families cannot effectively compete for starter homes with Wall Street firms and their vast resources,” Trump said in the order. “Neighborhoods and communities once controlled by middle-class American families are now run by faraway corporate interests. People live in homes, not corporations.”

Here is what to know about the executive order and how it will impact the housing market.

What does the order do?

Trump directed Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent to develop definitions for “large institutional investor” and “single-family home” within 30 days. 

Cabinet members, including Housing and Urban Development Secretary Scott Turner, must then issue guidance preventing the federal government from providing for, approving, insuring, guaranteeing, securitizing or facilitating the acquisition of a single-family home by a large institutional investor within 60 days. 

They also must issue guidance preventing the disposal of federal assets in a way that transfers a single-family home to a large institutional investor by that time. 

Attorney General Pam Bondi and Federal Trade Commission Chair Andrew Ferguson must also review “substantial acquisitions” of single-family homes in local markets by large institutional investors on antitrust grounds.

Turner must also require owners and managing agents of single-family homes participating in federal housing assistance programs to disclose direct or indirect owners, managers or affiliates to his department, which will then determine whether those entities constitute large institutional investors. 

The White House said Jan. 20 the order ensures federal housing programs “prioritize families, not Wall Street, and sets the stage for legislation to ensure that large institutional investors do not acquire single-family homes.” 

Why is Trump doing this now?

With less than 10 months before the midterms, Americans are persistently worried about the cost of living. A Wall Street Journal survey released earlier this month found that nearly 6 in 10 respondents have a negative view of the economy under Trump.

In December, the year-over-year cost of shelter was up 3.2 percent, with rent up 2.9 percent and estimated rent that owners would pay on their homes up 3.4 percent, according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics.

Public sentiment has soured in tandem with those higher costs. A survey conducted by the New York Federal Reserve last February found that renters put their average chances of owning a home at just less than 34 percent, down from more than 40 percent in February 2024 and nearly 52 percent in February 2020. 

“With a slowing labor market, consumers and households have become more concerned about their job security, which is causing them to at least pause before any major purchases like a home,” said Sean Fergus, the executive director for economic research at property analysis firm Zonda Home.

Amid those concerns, the president is embarking on a nationwide tour to tout his administration’s work on the economy, visiting Pennsylvania last month and Detroit earlier this month. 

Earlier this month, the president also directed Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to purchase $200 billion in mortgage-backed securities in an effort to lower mortgage rates. The National Association of Realtors (NAR) approved of the move but noted that “pressure on rates must be accompanied by efforts to expand supply.”

How many homes do Wall Street investors own?

Not enough to significantly skew home prices, according to Daryl Fairweather, chief economist at real estate brokerage Redfin. 

Fairweather told The Hill via email that homes purchased by investors increased by roughly 1 percent from 2024 to 2025, with 52,000 such purchases in the third quarter of last year. 

While that accounted for 17 percent of all homes purchased from July to September, homes purchased by Wall Street investors accounted for just 3 percent, she noted.

Fergus said “depending on the threshold used to classify as institutional vs. a mid-sized investor,” institutional investors own about 500,000 to 800,000 of the roughly 148.3 million housing units nationwide.  

The high end of that range accounts for around 0.5 percent of total units and less than 1 percent of the roughly 100 million single-family homes in the country, Fergus said. He also said more than 80 percent of single-family rentals, meanwhile, are owned by “mom-and-pop investors,” with institutional investors accounting for just 3 percent to 5 percent of single-family rentals. 

“Banning large investors or Wall Street investors likely wouldn’t make homebuying more affordable to regular buyers,” Fairweather said. “That’s because the kinds of homes that are attractive to large investors are also attractive to small investors because they see the same potential for rental income and home value appreciation.”

What other issues does the housing market face?

A supply shortage, first and foremost. 

The Census Bureau reported earlier this month that an estimated 488,000 homes were sold in October, roughly 8,000 more than projections from October 2024. 

But in December, there was 3.3 months’ worth of unsold inventory in the housing market, down from 4.2 months in November, according to the NAR. 

“The U.S. hasn’t built enough homes for years, especially affordable ones, and higher mortgage rates have made it harder for homeowners to sell,” Fairweather wrote. “That combination has left buyers with few options and kept prices elevated.”

That has contributed to an increase in housing prices that outpaces overall inflation. The median sale price of a home in December was $429,000, up by more than 28 percent relative to December 2020, according to data compiled by Redfin. 

The Treasury Department also said in 2024 that more than 90 percent of Americans lived in counties where median rent and house prices grew faster than median incomes from 2000-20.

Buddy Hughes, the chair of the National Association of Home Builders, said his organization appreciates Trump’s “efforts to create more opportunities for homeownership” but noted concerns over housing supply, which he said are driven by policies that “cut off capital for new construction.”

“With a lack of available housing the major cause of America’s housing affordability challenges, policy solutions must be focused on increasing our nation’s housing supply,” Hughes said in a statement. 

How can supply issues be addressed?

Fairweather and Fergus noted that policymakers should reduce the barriers for homebuilders, including speeding up the permitting process, and lower interest rates from the Federal Reserve, which would indirectly lower mortgage rates.

The Federal Open Market Committee is set to gather on Tuesday and Wednesday to decide whether to lower rates for the fourth straight meeting or hold them steady.

Fairweather also said government officials should encourage the construction of “smaller, more affordable homes” and allow for increased housing density. 

“Policies that focus only on limiting investors may miss the bigger picture. Without more supply, the affordability problem doesn’t go away—it just shifts to a different group of buyers,” she added.

At the local level, Fergus said that homebuilding regulations and fees should be reduced. Yet he noted that while such policy changes would “would lower new home construction costs and help drive more affordable supply,” they are “unlikely to happen in the near term.”

During testimony before the House Financial Services Committee on Jan. 21, Turner said up to 25 percent of the costs to build a single-family home come from regulations. 

“I have been traveling our country extensively, meeting with mayors, localities, planning and zoning professionals, economic development professionals, asking them and encouraging them too to take inventory of their regulatory environment, to unleash building and development in their said community,” he added.

data.title: GOP on defense over Noem's future as Congress nears shutdown deadline
data.description:
Kristi Noem’s continued leadership of the Department of Homeland Security is becoming a major obstacle to getting a bipartisan deal to avoid a government shutdown passed through the Senate.

A growing number of Senate Democrats are calling for Noem to be ousted, casting doubts on the prospect that they would agree to any concessions from President Trump to avoid a shutdown at the end of this week if Noem remains in charge of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE).

And one Republican, Sen. Thom Tillis (N.C.), said Tuesday that he’s lost faith in Noem’s ability to lead the department.

Asked if he still has confidence in Noem’s leadership, Tillis said: “None at all, none at all.”

“I think she’s out of her depth. She’s proven that. She doesn’t know how to lead, how to de-escalate. She’s exposing ICE officers to dangerous situations. She’s exposing U.S. citizens to deadly situations,” he told reporters.

Tillis said those concerns have been “communicated” to the White House.

Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (N.Y.), speaking on the Senate floor Tuesday, called Noem a “liar” and “incompetent” and urged Trump to “fire her at once before another American is killed.”

He reiterated his threat from the weekend that Democrats would block any government funding package that includes appropriations for the Department of Homeland Security, which includes ICE and U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP).

Senate Republicans aren’t doing much to defend Noem in the face of calls for her resignation from centrist Democrats who voted with them to end the 43-day shutdown last year, including Sens. John Fetterman (D-Pa.) and Jacky Rosen (D-Nev.).

Fetterman in a statement Tuesday compared Noem to former President Biden’s controversy-plagued head of Homeland Security, Alejandro Mayorkas.

“President Trump: I make a direct appeal to immediately fire Secretary Noem. Americans have died. She is betraying DHS’s core mission and trashing your border security legacy,” Fetterman said in a statement.

Asked Tuesday if he has confidence in Noem’s continued leadership of Homeland Security, Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) said, “That’s the president’s judgment call.”

Thune endorsed calls by other Senate Republicans for a thorough and independent investigation of Pretti’s death.

“We’ve seen a lot of video evidence already, and clearly there are a lot of questions that are going to have to be answered, and I think there’s an accounting that has to happen,” he said.

Key Republicans, such as Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.), the chair of the Senate Homeland Security Committee, and Rep. Mark Amodei (R-Nev.), the chair of the House Homeland Security Appropriations Subcommittee, appear increasingly frustrated with Noem’s leadership of the department.

Paul on Monday criticized Noem’s description of Alex Pretti, the 37-year-old nurse whom Border Patrol agents killed, as a “domestic terrorist” who wanted “to inflict maximum damage on individuals and kill law enforcement.”

“I can’t recall ever hearing a police chief immediately describing the victim as a ‘domestic terrorist’ or a ‘would-be assassin.’ For calm to be restored, an independent investigation is the least that should be done,” Paul posted on social media, also referring to rhetoric used by White House adviser Stephen Miller.

Amodei called for a Homeland Security officials to “pivot” given the strong public backlash to the fatal shootings of two protesters in Minneapolis this month.

“I’ll tell you this, there needs to be a pivot in the whole operation. Because regardless of what side immigration enforcement you’re on, we are not in a good place right now,” he said in an interview with The Nevada Independent.

An Economist/YouGov poll of 1,684 U.S. adults published Tuesday found that 55 percent of Americans say they have very little confidence in ICE, a 10 percent increase in the number of respondents who said so in December.

Senate Appropriations Committee Chair Susan Collins (R-Maine) said she asked Noem on Monday to “pause” immigration enforcement operations in Minnesota and Maine.

“I asked her to pause the operations in both Maine and Minnesota so that they could be reviewed and far more targeted in their scope,” Collins told Maine Public.

Collins disputed Noem’s description of Pretti as a “domestic terrorist” and questioned whether federal officers participating in Operation Metro Surge in Minneapolis have received adequate training.  

“I believe in a rush to get them out to the field that their training has not been as thorough and extensive as it should be,” she said.

Asked if he agrees with Collins call for a pause of ICE operations in Minnesota and Maine, Thune said the second fatal shooting of a protester in Minneapolis warrants a review of the Department of Homeland Security’s strategy and tactics.

“This is an inflection point, an opportunity to evaluate and really assess the policies and the procedures and how they’re being implemented and put into practice,” he said.

“I’m glad that Tom Homan is headed out there. I think he’s an experienced pro on these issues and has a lot of law enforcement experience and bringing that to bear in the situation there will be helpful,” he said.

Trump’s announcement Monday that he would send Homan, his border czar, to Minnesota and that he would report directly to the president was widely seen in Washington as a signal of a lack of confidence in Noem.

But Trump on Tuesday reiterated his support amid rumors that her days leading the Department of Homeland Security may be running out. Asked if Noem might resign, Trump told reporters: “No.”

“I think she’s doing a very good job. The border is totally secure. You know, you forget we had a border that I inherited where millions of people were coming through. Now we have a border where no one is coming through. They come into our country only legally,” he said.

While Trump held a “very good call” with Minnesota’s Democratic Gov. Tim Walz and recalled Greg Bovino, the controversial commander of the Border Patrol, from the operation in Minneapolis, Schumer said Tuesday he needs to go further to win over Democrats to the funding bill.

“There are reports that the president is thinking about a reduction in force. A reduction isn’t good enough. President Trump should act now to completely withdraw ICE and immediately turn down the temperature,” he said.

data.title: Iran's neighbors on edge as US 'armada' arrives in Middle East
data.description:
Iran’s neighbors are on edge over the possibility that President Trump will pull the trigger on strikes against Iran, as Tehran and its allies have promised to forcefully retaliate against U.S. positions, many of which are located inside the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Saudi Arabia. 

Regional powers, including the UAE, have reiterated that their airspace, territory and waters will not be permitted for use if military action against Iran takes place, but the arrival of the USS Abraham Lincoln and its accompanying destroyers in the U.S. Central Command (Centcom) area would allow Washington to go it alone, should Trump make good on his pledge to punish Tehran for its brutal crackdown against antiregime protesters. 

Trump has not taken diplomacy off the table, leaving channels open to negotiate over Iran’s nuclear program and ballistic missile stockpiles. But with no indication of an agreement in sight, security experts see the likelihood of U.S. strikes on Iran increasing.

Gregory Brew, an analyst and expert on Iran with the Eurasia Group, said all Gulf states are “very conscious” of the Iranian counterthreat and are therefore “trying to hedge against that retaliation.”

“They’re not stepping in to prevent U.S. action. They’re not getting in the way, but they’re signaling to Tehran that they won’t have a role,” Brew said in an interview with The Hill on Tuesday. 

Similar to the UAE, Saudi Arabia’s Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman told Iran’s President Masoud Pezeshkian during a call on Tuesday that the kingdom will not allow its airspace or land to be utilized in potential action against Iran. There are more than 2,300 U.S. service members stationed in Saudi Arabia. 

“This is an effort by regional states to manage their relationship with Tehran while also remaining on good terms with the United States by essentially signaling in both directions, we’re not taking a side in this,” Brew said. 

Michael Rubin, a former Pentagon official and currently a senior fellow at the American Enterprise Institute, said Iran’s neighbors are particularly concerned about their long-range ballistic missiles — and are “afraid” that Washington “won’t finish the job.” 

“When you have a hornet’s nest, you have two good options: Get rid of it or leave it alone. They are afraid the White House always tends to come down in the middle, lightly tapping it with a stick,” he told The Hill on Tuesday. 

Mohammad Akbarzadeh, a deputy political commander of the Islamic Revolution Guard Corps navy, told the state-aligned Fars News Agency that Iran’s neighbors have been informed that they are seen as friends, but he warned that if their airspace, land or waters are used in an attack against Tehran, they will be “regarded as hostile.”

Iranian proxies in Iraq, Yemen and Lebanon have also warned of retaliation against the U.S. should it strike Iran.

Trump has offered rhetorical support for the antiregime protesters in Iran, stating earlier this month that “Help is on its way.” But that help did not arrive before the hard-line regime deployed security forces to quell the protests, with human rights groups reporting that at least 6,000 people were killed in the crackdown. 

Adham Sahloul, a former defense official in the Biden administration, said there is a sense in Washington circles that the president might have boxed himself in where options against Iran are concerned.

“President Trump’s statements and force posture decisions, coupled with demonstrated credibility to back up words with kinetic action, means he may be setting himself up for his own Obama red line moment in the Middle East,” said Sahloul, now a senior fellow at the Center for a New American Security (CNAS). 

A White House official told The Hill on Tuesday that Trump is “watching” the situation in Iran “very seriously and all options are on the table if the regime executes protesters.” 

“As a result of President Trump’s warnings, Iranian protesters who were scheduled to be sentenced to death were not,” the official said in a statement. “As President Trump stated, he thinks this is good news and hopes this trend continues. He is watching very closely.” 

The administration has beefed up its firepower in the Middle East with the recent arrival of the USS Abraham Lincoln, which is equipped with Mk 57 Mod3 Sea Sparrow surface-to-air missile launchers and can carry squadrons of bombers and early warning jets, and its three complementary warships, to the Centcom area. The U.S. has also dispatched additional F-15s and cargo planes to the region, adding more strike options for the Pentagon, according to flight-tracking data. 

Retired U.S. Navy Vice Adm. Kevin Donegan said the arrival of the carrier strike group brings “quite a significant amount of military forces” to the region, with the carrier air wing consisting of eight squadrons of F-35C Lightning II, EA-18G Growlers, F/A-18E/F Super Hornets, MH-60R/S Sea Hawks and CMV-22B Ospreys, giving the president more “capability to carry out options” and better defend forces in the region. 

The U.S. forces currently in place could be used to degrade the capability of the internal security apparatus of the Iranians responsible for killing protesters and could deteriorate the nation’s ballistic missile system, said Donegan, who served as commander of the U.S. Navy’s Fifth Fleet. 

But Donegan noted that although the administration is positioning military assets in the region, the intent isn’t always to use them.

“These forces clearly have been also sent to increase the pressure on Iran to negotiate. So if you look at it from that standpoint, you know much better not to have to use the military forces in any way,” Donegan, now a distinguished senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, said in a Tuesday interview with The Hill. 

“Even if we were to use military force, the outcome of the use of that military force on a regime is very difficult to predict and I’d say no one has a crystal ball for that,” he said. 

The broader concern for Iran’s neighbors, namely Turkey, which shares a border with Iran, is if potential U.S. strikes transform into a protracted campaign that destabilizes the region, something Trump wants to avoid, said Brew of the Eurasia Group. 

Earlier this month, regional states including Saudi Arabia, Qatar and the UAE were lobbying the Trump administration against pulling the trigger on Iran, as there was not a significant U.S military presence in the region. But now the circumstances are different with the Lincoln being in the region, Brew said. 

“I think they’ve acknowledged that U.S. action is very likely, and that they are doing their best to distance themselves from that action,” he said. 

Trump has reportedly received multiple briefings in recent days indicating Iran’s government is at its weakest point in decades, facing deep public anger over the economy. 

Sahloul, of CNAS, argued that some see this period as an opportunity for Washington to “fundamentally alter the dynamics” in Iran. 

“That doesn’t necessarily mean regime change, but it might mean that the Islamic Republic as we know it won’t be the same menace to its own people and menace to the region, especially at this specific moment, possibly the weakest that the Islamic Republic has been — [it] has never been more geopolitically humbled,” he said.

data.title: Janet Yellen, Gary Locke to receive Elliot L. Richardson Prize
data.description:
Former Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen and former Washington Gov. Gary Locke (D) will receive the Elliot L. Richardson Prize for Excellence in Public Service at a Wednesday evening event.

The prize is intended to honor individuals “exemplifying the public service virtues demonstrated by the award’s namesake.”

Richardson is best remembered as the attorney general who resigned in 1973 rather than carry out former President Nixon’s order to fire the special prosecutor in the Watergate scandal. The episode is known as the “Saturday Night Massacre.”

Richardson — who served as secretary of Health, Education and Welfare (HEW); secretary of Defense; attorney general; and secretary of Commerce — is the only individual to have held four cabinet posts in history. He served under both Republican and Democratic presidents.

“Secretary Yellen and Governor Locke are pioneering public servants whose careers embody the spirit of service championed by Elliot Richardson,” said James-Christian Blockwood, the president and CEO of the National Academy of Public Administration, which administers the award.

“They exemplify a model of leadership rooted in commitment, excellence and service that will inspire future generations of public servants,” he continued.

Both public servants have held numerous high-profile positions in government.

Yellen is the former Treasury secretary, former chair of the Federal Reserve and former chair of the White House Council of Economic Advisers. She is the only woman to have held any of these posts and the only person to have held all three.

Locke served as the U.S. ambassador to China, Secretary of Commerce, and governor of Washington State. He is the first Chinese American to hold the latter two positions.

A press release ahead of the event touted both for their strong, innovative leadership and successful tenures.

Some past recipients include former Secretary of State Colin Powell, former Supreme Court Justice Sandra Day O’Connor, former Defense Secretary Leon Panetta and former director of the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases (NIAID) and former chief medical adviser Anthony Fauci.

data.title: Key takeaways from Minnesota special elections held amid immigration backlash
data.description:
Democrats handily won two special elections for state House seats in Minnesota, the first contests held in the state since growing backlash to the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown.

Voters in St. Paul and some of the city’s eastern suburbs elected Democrats Meg Luger-Nikolai and Shelly Buck for House Districts 64A and 47A, respectively.

Though both Democrats were widely expected to win their races, the elections were notable for coming at a turbulent time in Minnesota. Several days earlier, 37-year-old Alex Pretti was fatally shot by a member of the Border Patrol. Weeks before that, 37-year-old Renee Good was shot and killed by an ICE agent.

Here are key takeaways from the Minnesota special elections: 

State House is once again tied 

Democrats’ wins in both special elections means that the state House is now evenly split. As a result, the two parties will operate under their previously negotiated power-sharing agreement. 

That agreement allows Rep. Lisa Demuth (R) to be House Speaker while most House committees are co-chaired by both parties. 

Meanwhile, Democrats hold a narrow 34-33 edge in the state Senate. 

Control of both legislative chambers are expected to be hotly contested in November. While Democrats acknowledge that the party has to reconcile with the statewide fraud scandal that’s plagued the party, many Republicans admit the current situation with ICE in the state is untenable for its party. 

A decisive win for Democrats 

The two state House races were widely expected to remain in Democratic hands. In the end, there were no surprises on Tuesday night.

Shelley ran uncontested for District 47A in the eastern suburbs of St. Paul, where former Vice President Harris won with 61 percent of the vote, according to The Associated Press. 

Meanwhile, Harris won District 64A in St. Paul with over 83 percent of the vote. The Democrat on Tuesday night trounced her Republican opponent in the district. With 55 percent of the ballots counted that night, Luger-Nikolai was winning 95 percent of the vote.

The results, while expected, are sure to please Democrats, who are facing potentially competitive races for governor and Senate in November. 

Kicks off an eventful election year in Minnesota 

Tuesday’s elections mark the start of a hotly contested election year in the North Star State.  

Minnesota will be holding several competitive primaries for governor and Senate, and both parties are looking to expand their control in the legislature. 

Though a statewide fraud scandal has threatened to mire Democrats in November, the recent headlines have largely been overtaken by the two shootings.

The election results will test whether that scandal, as well as the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown, remain salient to the voters heading into November.  

At the same time, the midterm environment is shaping up to be much like 2018, which saw an anti-Trump blue wave. Democrats are hoping to harness that energy in order to flip not only the House but also the Senate.

data.title: Democrats win two Minnesota special elections, bringing state House back to a tie
data.description:
Democrats are projected to win special elections for two Minnesota House seats, bringing partisan control in the lower chamber back to a tie, according to Decision Desk HQ.

Democrats Shelley Buck and Meg Luger-Nikolai won special elections for state House Districts 47A and 64A, respectively. Buck ran uncontested in her special election.

District 47A includes portions of Maplewood and Woodbury, both located east of St. Paul, while District 64A is entirely within St. Paul.

The special elections bring the state House to a 67-67 tie, meaning the parties will operate under their previously agreed-upon power-sharing agreement. That includes keeping Rep. Lisa Demuth (R) as House Speaker while most House committees will be co-chaired by both parties.

The election results were widely expected, given both are blue-leaning districts. Yet the timing of the elections was notable, as they came amid growing backlash in the state to the Trump administration’s hardline immigration crackdown.

Just days earlier, 37-year-old Alex Pretti was shot and killed in Minneapolis by a member of the Border Patrol. His death came weeks after an ICE agent fatally shot 37-year-old Renee Good in Minneapolis.

The events have led to criticism from even some Republicans, and prompted GOP gubernatorial candidate Chris Madel to drop his bid for governor in Minnesota.

“With Representative-elect Buck and Luger-Nikolai’s victories, Minnesotans now have two more Democratic champions who will fight to lower costs, protect health care, and protect our community from federal overreach and violence,” Democratic National Committee (DNC) Ken Martin said in a statement lauding the wins.

data.title: Omar sprayed with unknown substance at Minneapolis town hall
data.description:
A man was tackled to the ground after spraying Rep. Ilhan Omar (D-Minn.) with an unknown substance during a town hall in her district on Tuesday.

A livestream of the incident showed the man standing and yelling at Omar, who was at the podium and calling for Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem to resign or face impeachment over her handling of the Minneapolis shootings. He was then seen spraying her, after which he was forced to the ground. Members of the crowd cheered as the man’s arms were tied behind his back.

Omar continued with the town hall after the incident.

“Here’s the reality that people like this ugly man don’t understand: We are Minnesota strong and we will stay resilient in the face of whatever they might throw at us,” Omar said following the incident.

The Hill has reached out to both Omar’s office and Minneapolis Police for comment.
